speaker: Robert Hieronimus
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: My name is Seth Crawford and I'm interviewing a Dr. Bob Horanamus. Due to the time constraint, we'll include the biographical information in the cover sheet. How did you find out about the Negro Leagues? Well, it started. My father was a sandlot coach. He told me about the Negro League players, how extraordinary they were, and all of the hell that they had to put up with took me to a game. And at that game up in Pennsylvania, it was the only Negro League game. It wasn't a Negro League game. It was a Negro League team playing a white championship team, which they beat terribly. I mean, they really wiped them out. And I was concerned for my safety because I'd heard other people say that fights broke out at these things. But there wasn't any problem with it. And the black players were most gracious in destroying this other team. And there were no fights except the people in the stands. The whites in the stands just went berserk when their team was, I think, 18 to three was the final score. It was like a little league baseball game. And that was the best the whites had at that time, put it up against us, one black team. So he taught me how different the game was. That is, if you got on first base in the Negro League since the pitchers were so good, I mean, they were really good. Tough. If you got on first base, it was like Biddle League. You better try to steal second. You better try to steal third. You couldn't depend on hits. You had to depend on quickness and brutality because it was a brutal game the way the black players played it. You know, they played for keeps. If they were going to be stealing a base, they were second base, for instance, there were some within that league that literally would cut people up deliberately. So to them, it was a sense of winning was everything because if the team that won made more money, it's that way today. But it's not on a daily basis like where these guys played. The team won that day when they got paid that day. Talk on more money. Describe the nature of your involvement with the Negro leagues. Members of the Negro leagues. Well, it started about 1991 when I started doing, I bumped into some, a card deck trading card set by Eclipse, which was a 36 card deck colored baseball cards, which gave the histories of the players. After reading their statistics and after reading, then getting hold of some other books, I began to realize that my father was saying that these guys were truly outstanding. And when I could never understand is why did most of them live in such horrendous circumstances? Why wasn't baseball taking care of them? I was naive because I didn't know that white major league baseball didn't even consider these players at all. And that's what really burned me internally to attempt to go to other sports people because I didn't feel capable of being in the media. I wasn't a sports person. So I didn't feel capable of talking to interviewing these players and dad and dad and dad and dad. So I went to other people, big shots in this town, a little town of Baltimore. And unfortunately, what does it say? Unfortunately, the reaction of the sports casters was something I had a very uneasy feeling about because then I realized that most of them were just like high school kids. They were very immature, not concerned about anything concerning rights of individuals. And it was a major blow to me when I talked to them and found out that they espoused ideas like all the league players weren't that good because when they played against white teams, white teams beat them pretty bad. I mean, it was like no contest. And a lot of other disinformation like that, the little remarks like all they played with inferior baseballs and inferior bats and inferior gloves, well, my heavens, that's not a case against them. The fact is, when they were hitting 600 foot home runs off of frozen baseballs and things of that nature because baseballs were doctored a lot in the Negro leagues, it seemed to me that they were even better than the players they were playing against. And then I started reading the research because I was always told that there was very little known about them. Well, since the mid-70s, books of all types began to be published, but it wasn't until the late 80s, early 90s that we really got some real gems on the history of the Negro leagues and some of the records. And that's when I, after receiving that material, I took it to other sports people and said, you know, you told me these guys played in these little band boxes and anybody could have only hit a home run. What's Griffith Stadium doing in here? Why are they hitting the ball out of Griffith Stadium? How can this be? That's the, that was the largest stadium on a mile of memory, 500 some feet center field and they're hitting it out of the park. Now you tell me they're not good. And then I pointed to the records, which were, which I had not known earlier, what happened when the Negro League players played against white Major League All-Star teams? Well, they won two out of three games. What does that mean? Does that mean they're inferior? All I was basically trying to say was they were at least as good as. But what I was finding out was they were more than good and they were more than at least as good as many times and most times they were better. Now I began to realize that this must rub some announcers the wrong way. And I talked to all kinds of individuals all the way up to, no, we know this is Alan, Alan, who became a friend of mine years later. No, was it Neil Alan? No. Oh, for the New York Yankees, he was their, he was their announcer, their games. And recalls Neil Alan, but Alan was, was last thing, I believe it was. So after being initiated into the fact that most, most guys who were announcers at baseball and got involved in it, were ignorant of this area and that they weren't going to do anything because I didn't know the politics. There's a lot of politics in sports. Being friends with certain individuals makes it, it makes, puts you in a situation where you can do or adult things. If you, if you say friends, since our friends with that person and they make brevious mistakes in the field, but you never mentioned it, of course it's going to bond that friendship. If your friends with them and they're making terrible mistakes and you pointed out, you put that relationship in jeopardy. Now most people think that their professionals can handle that, but that's not, that's not what those on in the locker room. You know, so I was beginning to learn a lot of things about sports world, especially in broadcasting that I really didn't want to know because I had my own images in my head as how I thought it was. I had no idea these guys were so poorly schooled, didn't read anything except sport pages and had egos bigger than this house. And this is a big house. So as far as I was concerned, I felt like the trade by the people in the sports area because going to them made no difference whatsoever. Every one of them said something like, isn't that too bad or, wow, I wish we could do something, well they could do something, but they weren't going to. It wasn't in enough, there wasn't enough pressure, especially coming from myself who was not heavily into sports in the sports world. So I left, I just dropped it from 91 to about 93 or 4. We kept going back to these guys, sending them material, it made no difference because they, we know they didn't read it by then, but we wanted to make sure that they realized that Leon Day was coming up for, once again, for induction into the Hall of Fame and we needed their help. We needed them to do stories on it and because there was a lot of activity that was taking place around Leon, especially with the Veterans Committee, the Hall of Fame Committee. They were attempting to do what they could to, he should have been in in 93. I think I discussed this with you before in 1993. The heartbreaker of 1993 was, he had all the votes, you needed 12, he had them. The one vote that couldn't attend was War of Campanella. Now, War of Campanella has been ill and was ill for years, years. They knew where his vote was going to go, but for some peculiar reason, they wouldn't allow him to cast it because he couldn't attend that meeting. Why that was, I'll never be able to figure it out, but it was in 1993 that Leon lost hope in a sense that he didn't think there was a chance in hell of his getting in because usually what happens is they'll push a front runner for several years. And if he doesn't make it regardless of his quality, there may be political problems inside and all this other kind of thing within the voting, they usually just drop it. And that's what Leon basically thought was going to happen. But we told him that basically we weren't going to stop pushing because, I mean, how can you not push for a guy who has the winningest percentage of as a pitcher and hit three over three hundred? I mean, how could you not push a guy like that, especially pitching against the kind of people that he was pitching against, pitching against central page? I mean, there aren't, I don't know of any white, major league pitcher that could go up and his team could go up, going up against central page in his prime and beat him. He was just impossible to beat, throwing the ball at 100 miles an hour consistently and was such accuracy that when he used to practice and you hear a lot of this from the stories they tell, they would sit little pins and matchsticks up at the plate and he would knock these down precisely, no matter where they put them. So that speed was one important thing, but his accuracy was just uncanny. And he got that, as he said, and growing up when he was growing up, he had to kill rocks on the squirrels for food and he threw it by throwing these rocks at the animals. He was very, very adept at it. So I think I brought it up to about 94, right? Yeah. But of course I deviated from your question, and I didn't mean to. That's okay. But I wanted to get in as much as possible. Where would you like to take it from here? I'd like to know who or some of your chief adversaries as far as getting Lee on day into the olive family. Like who did you, who was opposed to it? Opposed to it is not the right word. Ignore it and say it's not important. Every major baseball announcer on WBAL and even on my station at that time, WCBM. Because they all spoused the same crap. They never played against my teams and when they did, they got the hell kicked out of them. When they played in stadiums, they played in stadiums that were band boxes. So who in the hell couldn't hit the ball out of that? They literally believed all this intergossip. So you're talking, I can't recall their names right now, but anyone would know their names. Who was the man that was the Orioles broadcaster on BAL until he was fired just recently? I can't recall his name. I have a terrible habit of having had so much opposition in my life in just about any area that I work in that I forget about the names of the people because I don't want to get personally involved. John Miller. John Miller was not, I remember going to John Miller just before Lee on, this is in February 1995 and they were having the big, big celebration out in front of the Bay Brewton's gym. And I said, you know, Neon Day is going to make it in. He said, oh, isn't it Leon Day dead? He said, no, he's not dead. He's going to make it in the Hall of Fame. He says, how do you know? I said, well, I was told in a long time ago, and of course I didn't tell him who told me, which was Buck O'Neill. Buck O'Neill called me in the spring of 1994 and said the deal was done since they had gotten Phil Rizudo in and they had gotten one other player in. That's how the Hall of Fame voting works because these deals that have been set up, which is really sad, especially for a person whose family adored baseball. I mean, to think that the things are deals and they're not rigged, but it's not as the way we always thought it was, which was God. I was so good he was voted in. No, there was all kinds of political pressure applied. But anyway, Neon, when I told him that Neon was definitely going to get in, he didn't believe me, of course. And I gave up. That was the last time I talked to John Miller on the subject. I tried talking to him before and that, but there were others that were... Every sports person, well, I can think of until about 1993, the 1994, wouldn't have anything to do with this. What broke the ice in part was in 1992, the Baltimore Orioles had a Leon Day Day in September. That was the first time he was recognized by the Oriole organization in a large open way. He had been recognized before by Calvin Hill, who used to be with the Orioles, who was a great half-backer, I believe, for the Dallas Cowboys at one time. I'm not sure how it would be spoken to me on that. I'm not that good at that stuff. But Calvin Hill literally was a force within that organization that educated people about the Black and Black players. And thank heavens he was black. Thank heavens he was successful. But we expected, we expected other Black players to get on board. We had hoped that Frank Robinson would give support. Frank Robinson. Frank Robinson was the most ignorant of all of the players I've ever met in regards to this. He could have cared less about the... He actually denounced the Negro League players at one time. He actually said that there were no Negro League players that deserved to be in the Hall of Fame. That is about as dumb as, you know, it can go. Frank Robinson was the most ignorant person player I've... Black player. I've ever encountered this whole ordeal. As a matter of fact, it wasn't until... He didn't say one positive thing about Dion Day until he was forced to when Dion Day got into the Hall of Fame and we had to get off the Cooperstown early to make things up and everything like that for his wife Geraldine who was coming up and everything. And what happened was, these players, I mean they were... They were celebrating at... I think it was in the afternoon or something, Seth. And what happened was that Hammond, I think his name is Dick Hammond. I'm not certain of the name, but he was a big man in the oral organization a couple of years ago. They needed a black person to introduce Dion Day that was a player. And of course they hadn't prepared for it enough and so they turned around to Frank and Hammond asked him to do it and he wasn't going to do it. And Hammond says you're going to do it. And he did it. But it was only under enormous pressure. And because of that pressure, it left a very bad taste in Geraldine's mouth because she saw this as it was happening. You can imagine how embarrassing it is for certain individuals to watch something like that having to be persuaded to do something against their will. So yes, we're in the middle of an interview so girls go back there please. Okay. I guess what worked when... On meeting these legally players that should help provide compensation, but were the conditions that they were living in prior to your help. As far as I guess... Oh, you know, this is embarrassing for them. And you want the truth. You don't want me to be asked here. And they are my friends and so I know that what I'm going to say to you right now is not going to embarrass them the way it would other people would if it embarrassed them. I mean, there were times, I'll give you an example, there were times when Leon and his wife had no food at all. And when they asked for some help from his sister, he was passed on since she helped them a little, but then they even dog food. They were eating dog food. They were living in places and this isn't so much Leon and Geraldine, but others that I had visited that had no heat. That didn't... I mean, you went through the refrigerator and there was old things sitting there that would never be eaten because they were... Get going bad. In the winter time they had these little schedules of when they would turn the heat on and when they would turn it off. And to see men 65 and 70 and 75 years old, not with... Forget about adequate food. Food. Any kind of food, whether it's even a hotdog or a cheeseburger. No way. It was that kind of a thing that shook me so hard that I realized that I didn't want to embarrass them, but at the same time I had to say something about it. And that's when I got in touch with some of the... What would be called today, the movers and the shakers. That is the founders of the Negro League Baseball Players Association. And they were trying to help these guys the same way I was, but they didn't have the money. How expensive was the process, would you say? If you could... In doing this whole thing? Over 100,000 dollars. Since 93 to 92, something around there. Because what we had to do, it wasn't just giving them money outright. That's not really what happened. We would do fundraisers. We would do things that would educate people at the same time. But the problem in doing a fund raiser is that all the money you're putting into fundraisers to educate could go directly to the people. But then that needed help. But the problem with that is, of course, then still the problem goes on and on because everyone else doesn't know about it. So whenever you throw one of these dinners or you throw one of these things, you know, you're talking tens of thousands of dollars, which sometimes the process of educating them at the same time of doing something for them, it was a nip and tuk thing. We took... We had to make some loans. We had to stretch ourselves in other areas. And we did it in a very carefully thought-out way so that one loan didn't get screwed up with another loan. We paid off one loan and in time to make another loan to do something else. Because this kind of money doesn't sound like much money. It doesn't. I mean, the way things are hard today, a hundred thousand dollars sounds like... But it was hard. It was really difficult, especially in the sense that when you're trying to educate people on this, we had to educate not just sports people, but people within our family, people that were our friends. They thought we were crazy. The first thing they said, Seth, was, but you're white. They're not going to care about what you do. They won't care what you... You know, it doesn't make any difference. That's their problem. Let the blacks take care of the blacks. And we were looking at it that way. We were looking at... This is going to sound like Eve is hell. We were looking at these as people. These were people doing this. These were humans. They were going this. And they were great ball players, greater than the guys that are around on the field. Why? Did that... Must they go through this? Somebody had to do something. So we figured, hey, if we do this, then other people will get involved in doing this. Well, that was kind of naive. Because other people, for some reason, after people see you on television for a while, listen to you on a radio. And they think you're famous or they think you have a lot of money. They see these players on... We'd interview them on the radio. They'd see them on television because we'd drag them in front of the cameras and everything. Thank God for Marty Bass and Don Scott and Dan Roberts and another fellow on WMAR TV. Basically, it was WMAR TV and WMAR TV and WMAR JZ TV, those two stations that made all the difference in the world. Because once these people started seeing these guys, they'd said, hey, wow, that's a real major league player. He's real. You know, these guys were becoming legends all over the country. But it didn't change their income, Seth. That's the problem. Because being on TV doesn't make any money. People began to think, oh, they're successful now. That's great. So when they'd see them, when they'd see them and they'd sign autographs, they'd try this buddy-buddy thing. And it's an easy thing to do with people who have got any recognition. They'd come up to them and they'd say, well, I saw you on television. You were like, praise me. And I got a child here and they want to say, hello to you. And we'd just sign this and the guys are just too embarrassed to ask for a signing fee. That's not true now. But then they were overwhelmed that people were paying attention to them. And that was enough to ask for money from their perspective. It was like, that's a no-no. I don't want to do that. I mean, besides, they were giving people the difference between these players. People never see the like of this again. Said, these were players that were better than their counter-carts. These teams were like 14 and 15 people. They never had more than 15 people because they couldn't afford it. Everyone was in competition with everyone else. One guy pitched and then next day he was in the outfield, first base. They played all around. No one day didn't have any. What do they call designated hears? You know, that was a joke. What do you mean you can't hit? You're playing baseball and you can't hit. So people stopped to think that they were successful and had money. And then they started to ignore them again and take advantage of them. They would be invited to places and they didn't even have enough money to get to go to those places. It was that kind of ignorance and because unfortunately, just because the guy wears his best suit to go on television and looks really good and happy and doesn't mean that he's going to go home and eat. Can you name some of the players for him who helped provide assistance? What besides Leon Day? Well, yeah, sure. Jimmy Crutchfield, say for instance, you've probably never heard of Jimmy. He died in the early 90s after we took him to the White House and spent some time with George Bush. It's ironic that the Republican administration has done more for these players than Clinton administration. Has done. I never thought about it in this way. There has to be at least 20 some 20, 25 from Josh Gibson Jr. to what would happen is that we get a phone call and they would say we don't have heat or it's got to go to the hospital. Or what we do would do is we make sure that that was fulfilled. But after a while, I mean after a while, some of these players, almost every one of them were such gentlemen set. But there were always just a few who liked with everybody else. When they saw somebody else gain a little bit like Leon. When Leon passed on, this was in February of 1995 when I was talking to him. He said he was very concerned about his wife. He was very concerned about what's going to happen when I'm gone. I don't have any money. They're going to take the house. They're going to not his house, the apartment. And there's true he didn't even have enough money to be buried in in the simplest way. So are attempts to do and help them, you've only won something? Okay. Yes please. Just be quiet. I remember when I was a child. That's the problem of being interfered with. I just can't remember where I am. I'm talking about how some people, when they saw Leon Day, getting compensation or getting anything. Oh, some of the players thought, why aren't you doing it for me? What's an impossibility? You can't help every one of them. And that's the reason why we help put on card shows and that type of thing. These things are very expensive to put on and very little money is ever made. You put on something that cost 10,000 to put on and then you turn over $350 to the player. I mean, that's how bad it was because Baltimore was not like Chicago or New York or Philadelphia where there was a better relationship with the media and the press because the guys in Chicago and Pittsburgh and New York, they knew about the history of it. They knew they were much more intelligent than our sports casters here. They read, you know, I didn't have to give them. I mean, we spent thousands of dollars just in providing books to put on the shelves of all these guys in the media in this town. And they looked at the pictures. I was told and they showed us how to read them. They looked at the pictures. So it's that kind of thing in which when you begin to help, you always have the problem of how far do you go because you've got to get other people involved in it, Seth, in some way, in to educate them. And there's the rub. It's not just like, if I had $100,000 to put into it, you do that. It is the work that needs to be done along with the money. And there's no way to compensate that aspect of it because it took months of early...

segments.text: My name is Seth Crawford and I'm interviewing a Dr. Bob Horanamus.
Due to the time constraint, we'll include the biographical information in the cover sheet.
How did you find out about the Negro Leagues?
Well, it started.
My father was a sandlot coach.
He told me about the Negro League players, how extraordinary they were, and all of the
hell that they had to put up with took me to a game.
And at that game up in Pennsylvania, it was the only Negro League game.
It wasn't a Negro League game.
It was a Negro League team playing a white championship team, which they beat terribly.
I mean, they really wiped them out.
And I was concerned for my safety because I'd heard other people say that fights broke
out at these things.
But there wasn't any problem with it.
And the black players were most gracious in destroying this other team.
And there were no fights except the people in the stands.
The whites in the stands just went berserk when their team was, I think, 18 to three was
the final score.
It was like a little league baseball game.
And that was the best the whites had at that time, put it up against us, one black team.
So he taught me how different the game was.
That is, if you got on first base in the Negro League since the pitchers were so good, I
mean, they were really good.
Tough.
If you got on first base, it was like Biddle League.
You better try to steal second.
You better try to steal third.
You couldn't depend on hits.
You had to depend on quickness and brutality because it was a brutal game the way the black
players played it.
You know, they played for keeps.
If they were going to be stealing a base, they were second base, for instance, there
were some within that league that literally would cut people up deliberately.
So to them, it was a sense of winning was everything because if the team that won made
more money, it's that way today.
But it's not on a daily basis like where these guys played.
The team won that day when they got paid that day.
Talk on more money.
Describe the nature of your involvement with the Negro leagues.
Members of the Negro leagues.
Well, it started about 1991 when I started doing, I bumped into some, a card deck trading
card set by Eclipse, which was a 36 card deck colored baseball cards, which gave the
histories of the players.
After reading their statistics and after reading, then getting hold of some other books, I began
to realize that my father was saying that these guys were truly outstanding.
And when I could never understand is why did most of them live in such horrendous circumstances?
Why wasn't baseball taking care of them?
I was naive because I didn't know that white major league baseball didn't even consider
these players at all.
And that's what really burned me internally to attempt to go to other sports people because
I didn't feel capable of being in the media.
I wasn't a sports person.
So I didn't feel capable of talking to interviewing these players and dad and dad and dad and
dad.
So I went to other people, big shots in this town, a little town of Baltimore.
And unfortunately, what does it say?
Unfortunately, the reaction of the sports casters was something I had a very uneasy feeling
about because then I realized that most of them were just like high school kids.
They were very immature, not concerned about anything concerning rights of individuals.
And it was a major blow to me when I talked to them and found out that they espoused ideas
like all the league players weren't that good because when they played against white teams,
white teams beat them pretty bad.
I mean, it was like no contest.
And a lot of other disinformation like that, the little remarks like all they played with
inferior baseballs and inferior bats and inferior gloves, well, my heavens, that's not a case
against them.
The fact is, when they were hitting 600 foot home runs off of frozen baseballs and things
of that nature because baseballs were doctored a lot in the Negro leagues, it seemed to
me that they were even better than the players they were playing against.
And then I started reading the research because I was always told that there was very little
known about them.
Well, since the mid-70s, books of all types began to be published, but it wasn't until
the late 80s, early 90s that we really got some real gems on the history of the Negro
leagues and some of the records.
And that's when I, after receiving that material, I took it to other sports people and said,
you know, you told me these guys played in these little band boxes and anybody could
have only hit a home run.
What's Griffith Stadium doing in here?
Why are they hitting the ball out of Griffith Stadium?
How can this be?
That's the, that was the largest stadium on a mile of memory, 500 some feet center field
and they're hitting it out of the park.
Now you tell me they're not good.
And then I pointed to the records, which were, which I had not known earlier, what happened
when the Negro League players played against white Major League All-Star teams?
Well, they won two out of three games.
What does that mean?
Does that mean they're inferior?
All I was basically trying to say was they were at least as good as.
But what I was finding out was they were more than good and they were more than at least
as good as many times and most times they were better.
Now I began to realize that this must rub some announcers the wrong way.
And I talked to all kinds of individuals all the way up to, no, we know this is Alan,
Alan, who became a friend of mine years later.
No, was it Neil Alan?
No.
Oh, for the New York Yankees, he was their, he was their announcer, their games.
And recalls Neil Alan, but Alan was, was last thing, I believe it was.
So after being initiated into the fact that most, most guys who were announcers at baseball
and got involved in it, were ignorant of this area and that they weren't going to do
anything because I didn't know the politics.
There's a lot of politics in sports.
Being friends with certain individuals makes it, it makes, puts you in a situation where
you can do or adult things.
If you, if you say friends, since our friends with that person and they make brevious mistakes
in the field, but you never mentioned it, of course it's going to bond that friendship.
If your friends with them and they're making terrible mistakes and you pointed out, you
put that relationship in jeopardy.
Now most people think that their professionals can handle that, but that's not, that's
not what those on in the locker room.
You know, so I was beginning to learn a lot of things about sports world, especially
in broadcasting that I really didn't want to know because I had my own images in my head
as how I thought it was.
I had no idea these guys were so poorly schooled, didn't read anything except sport pages
and had egos bigger than this house.
And this is a big house.
So as far as I was concerned, I felt like the trade by the people in the sports area
because going to them made no difference whatsoever.
Every one of them said something like, isn't that too bad or, wow, I wish we could do
something, well they could do something, but they weren't going to.
It wasn't in enough, there wasn't enough pressure, especially coming from myself who was
not heavily into sports in the sports world.
So I left, I just dropped it from 91 to about 93 or 4.
We kept going back to these guys, sending them material, it made no difference because
they, we know they didn't read it by then, but we wanted to make sure that they realized
that Leon Day was coming up for, once again, for induction into the Hall of Fame and we
needed their help.
We needed them to do stories on it and because there was a lot of activity that was taking
place around Leon, especially with the Veterans Committee, the Hall of Fame Committee.
They were attempting to do what they could to, he should have been in in 93.
I think I discussed this with you before in 1993.
The heartbreaker of 1993 was, he had all the votes, you needed 12, he had them.
The one vote that couldn't attend was War of Campanella.
Now, War of Campanella has been ill and was ill for years, years.
They knew where his vote was going to go, but for some peculiar reason, they wouldn't
allow him to cast it because he couldn't attend that meeting.
Why that was, I'll never be able to figure it out, but it was in 1993 that Leon lost hope
in a sense that he didn't think there was a chance in hell of his getting in because
usually what happens is they'll push a front runner for several years.
And if he doesn't make it regardless of his quality, there may be political problems
inside and all this other kind of thing within the voting, they usually just drop it.
And that's what Leon basically thought was going to happen.
But we told him that basically we weren't going to stop pushing because, I mean, how can
you not push for a guy who has the winningest percentage of as a pitcher and hit three over
three hundred?
I mean, how could you not push a guy like that, especially pitching against the kind of
people that he was pitching against, pitching against central page?
I mean, there aren't, I don't know of any white, major league pitcher that could go up and
his team could go up, going up against central page in his prime and beat him.
He was just impossible to beat, throwing the ball at 100 miles an hour consistently and
was such accuracy that when he used to practice and you hear a lot of this from the stories
they tell, they would sit little pins and matchsticks up at the plate and he would knock these
down precisely, no matter where they put them.
So that speed was one important thing, but his accuracy was just uncanny.
And he got that, as he said, and growing up when he was growing up, he had to kill
rocks on the squirrels for food and he threw it by throwing these rocks at the animals.
He was very, very adept at it.
So I think I brought it up to about 94, right?
Yeah.
But of course I deviated from your question, and I didn't mean to.
That's okay.
But I wanted to get in as much as possible.
Where would you like to take it from here?
I'd like to know who or some of your chief adversaries as far as getting Lee on day
into the olive family.
Like who did you, who was opposed to it?
Opposed to it is not the right word.
Ignore it and say it's not important.
Every major baseball announcer on WBAL and even on my station at that time, WCBM.
Because they all spoused the same crap.
They never played against my teams and when they did, they got the hell kicked out of them.
When they played in stadiums, they played in stadiums that were band boxes.
So who in the hell couldn't hit the ball out of that?
They literally believed all this intergossip.
So you're talking, I can't recall their names right now, but anyone would know their names.
Who was the man that was the Orioles broadcaster on BAL until he was fired just recently?
I can't recall his name.
I have a terrible habit of having had so much opposition in my life in just about any
area that I work in that I forget about the names of the people because I don't want
to get personally involved.
John Miller.
John Miller was not, I remember going to John Miller just before Lee on, this is in February
1995 and they were having the big, big celebration out in front of the Bay Brewton's gym.
And I said, you know, Neon Day is going to make it in.
He said, oh, isn't it Leon Day dead?
He said, no, he's not dead.
He's going to make it in the Hall of Fame.
He says, how do you know?
I said, well, I was told in a long time ago, and of course I didn't tell him who told
me, which was Buck O'Neill.
Buck O'Neill called me in the spring of 1994 and said the deal was done since they had
gotten Phil Rizudo in and they had gotten one other player in.
That's how the Hall of Fame voting works because these deals that have been set up, which
is really sad, especially for a person whose family adored baseball.
I mean, to think that the things are deals and they're not rigged, but it's not as the
way we always thought it was, which was God.
I was so good he was voted in.
No, there was all kinds of political pressure applied.
But anyway, Neon, when I told him that Neon was definitely going to get in, he didn't
believe me, of course.
And I gave up.
That was the last time I talked to John Miller on the subject.
I tried talking to him before and that, but there were others that were...
Every sports person, well, I can think of until about 1993, the 1994, wouldn't have anything
to do with this.
What broke the ice in part was in 1992, the Baltimore Orioles had a Leon Day Day in September.
That was the first time he was recognized by the Oriole organization in a large open
way.
He had been recognized before by Calvin Hill, who used to be with the Orioles, who was
a great half-backer, I believe, for the Dallas Cowboys at one time.
I'm not sure how it would be spoken to me on that.
I'm not that good at that stuff.
But Calvin Hill literally was a force within that organization that educated people about
the Black and Black players.
And thank heavens he was black.
Thank heavens he was successful.
But we expected, we expected other Black players to get on board.
We had hoped that Frank Robinson would give support.
Frank Robinson.
Frank Robinson was the most ignorant of all of the players I've ever met in regards to
this.
He could have cared less about the...
He actually denounced the Negro League players at one time.
He actually said that there were no Negro League players that deserved to be in the Hall
of Fame.
That is about as dumb as, you know, it can go.
Frank Robinson was the most ignorant person player I've...
Black player.
I've ever encountered this whole ordeal.
As a matter of fact, it wasn't until...
He didn't say one positive thing about Dion Day until he was forced to when Dion Day
got into the Hall of Fame and we had to get off the Cooperstown early to make things
up and everything like that for his wife Geraldine who was coming up and everything.
And what happened was, these players, I mean they were...
They were celebrating at...
I think it was in the afternoon or something, Seth.
And what happened was that Hammond, I think his name is Dick Hammond.
I'm not certain of the name, but he was a big man in the oral organization a couple
of years ago.
They needed a black person to introduce Dion Day that was a player.
And of course they hadn't prepared for it enough and so they turned around to Frank and
Hammond asked him to do it and he wasn't going to do it.
And Hammond says you're going to do it.
And he did it.
But it was only under enormous pressure.
And because of that pressure, it left a very bad taste in Geraldine's mouth because she
saw this as it was happening.
You can imagine how embarrassing it is for certain individuals to watch something like
that having to be persuaded to do something against their will.
So yes, we're in the middle of an interview so girls go back there please.
Okay.
I guess what worked when...
On meeting these legally players that should help provide compensation, but were the conditions
that they were living in prior to your help.
As far as I guess...
Oh, you know, this is embarrassing for them.
And you want the truth.
You don't want me to be asked here.
And they are my friends and so I know that what I'm going to say to you right now is
not going to embarrass them the way it would other people would if it embarrassed them.
I mean, there were times, I'll give you an example, there were times when Leon and his
wife had no food at all.
And when they asked for some help from his sister, he was passed on since she helped them
a little, but then they even dog food.
They were eating dog food.
They were living in places and this isn't so much Leon and Geraldine, but others that
I had visited that had no heat.
That didn't...
I mean, you went through the refrigerator and there was old things sitting there that
would never be eaten because they were...
Get going bad.
In the winter time they had these little schedules of when they would turn the heat on and when
they would turn it off.
And to see men 65 and 70 and 75 years old, not with...
Forget about adequate food.
Food.
Any kind of food, whether it's even a hotdog or a cheeseburger.
No way.
It was that kind of a thing that shook me so hard that I realized that I didn't want
to embarrass them, but at the same time I had to say something about it.
And that's when I got in touch with some of the...
What would be called today, the movers and the shakers.
That is the founders of the Negro League Baseball Players Association.
And they were trying to help these guys the same way I was, but they didn't have the
money.
How expensive was the process, would you say?
If you could...
In doing this whole thing?
Over 100,000 dollars.
Since 93 to 92, something around there.
Because what we had to do, it wasn't just giving them money outright.
That's not really what happened.
We would do fundraisers.
We would do things that would educate people at the same time.
But the problem in doing a fund raiser is that all the money you're putting into fundraisers
to educate could go directly to the people.
But then that needed help.
But the problem with that is, of course, then still the problem goes on and on because
everyone else doesn't know about it.
So whenever you throw one of these dinners or you throw one of these things, you know,
you're talking tens of thousands of dollars, which sometimes the process of educating them
at the same time of doing something for them, it was a nip and tuk thing.
We took...
We had to make some loans.
We had to stretch ourselves in other areas.
And we did it in a very carefully thought-out way so that one loan didn't get screwed
up with another loan.
We paid off one loan and in time to make another loan to do something else.
Because this kind of money doesn't sound like much money.
It doesn't.
I mean, the way things are hard today, a hundred thousand dollars sounds like...
But it was hard.
It was really difficult, especially in the sense that when you're trying to educate people
on this, we had to educate not just sports people, but people within our family, people
that were our friends.
They thought we were crazy.
The first thing they said, Seth, was, but you're white.
They're not going to care about what you do.
They won't care what you...
You know, it doesn't make any difference.
That's their problem.
Let the blacks take care of the blacks.
And we were looking at it that way.
We were looking at...
This is going to sound like Eve is hell.
We were looking at these as people.
These were people doing this.
These were humans.
They were going this.
And they were great ball players, greater than the guys that are around on the field.
Why?
Did that...
Must they go through this?
Somebody had to do something.
So we figured, hey, if we do this, then other people will get involved in doing this.
Well, that was kind of naive.
Because other people, for some reason, after people see you on television for a while,
listen to you on a radio.
And they think you're famous or they think you have a lot of money.
They see these players on...
We'd interview them on the radio.
They'd see them on television because we'd drag them in front of the cameras and everything.
Thank God for Marty Bass and Don Scott and Dan Roberts and another fellow on WMAR TV.
Basically, it was WMAR TV and WMAR TV and WMAR JZ TV, those two stations that made
all the difference in the world.
Because once these people started seeing these guys, they'd said, hey, wow, that's a real
major league player.
He's real.
You know, these guys were becoming legends all over the country.
But it didn't change their income, Seth.
That's the problem.
Because being on TV doesn't make any money.
People began to think, oh, they're successful now.
That's great.
So when they'd see them, when they'd see them and they'd sign autographs, they'd try this
buddy-buddy thing.
And it's an easy thing to do with people who have got any recognition.
They'd come up to them and they'd say, well, I saw you on television.
You were like, praise me.
And I got a child here and they want to say, hello to you.
And we'd just sign this and the guys are just too embarrassed to ask for a signing fee.
That's not true now.
But then they were overwhelmed that people were paying attention to them.
And that was enough to ask for money from their perspective.
It was like, that's a no-no.
I don't want to do that.
I mean, besides, they were giving people the difference between these players.
People never see the like of this again.
Said, these were players that were better than their counter-carts.
These teams were like 14 and 15 people.
They never had more than 15 people because they couldn't afford it.
Everyone was in competition with everyone else.
One guy pitched and then next day he was in the outfield, first base.
They played all around.
No one day didn't have any.
What do they call designated hears?
You know, that was a joke.
What do you mean you can't hit?
You're playing baseball and you can't hit.
So people stopped to think that they were successful and had money.
And then they started to ignore them again and take advantage of them.
They would be invited to places and they didn't even have enough money to get to go to those places.
It was that kind of ignorance and because unfortunately, just because the guy wears his best suit to go on television
and looks really good and happy and doesn't mean that he's going to go home and eat.
Can you name some of the players for him who helped provide assistance?
What besides Leon Day?
Well, yeah, sure.
Jimmy Crutchfield, say for instance, you've probably never heard of Jimmy.
He died in the early 90s after we took him to the White House and spent some time with George Bush.
It's ironic that the Republican administration has done more for these players than Clinton administration.
Has done.
I never thought about it in this way.
There has to be at least 20 some 20, 25 from Josh Gibson Jr. to what would happen is
that we get a phone call and they would say we don't have heat or it's got to go to the hospital.
Or what we do would do is we make sure that that was fulfilled.
But after a while, I mean after a while, some of these players, almost every one of them were such gentlemen set.
But there were always just a few who liked with everybody else.
When they saw somebody else gain a little bit like Leon.
When Leon passed on, this was in February of 1995 when I was talking to him.
He said he was very concerned about his wife.
He was very concerned about what's going to happen when I'm gone.
I don't have any money.
They're going to take the house. They're going to not his house, the apartment.
And there's true he didn't even have enough money to be buried in in the simplest way.
So are attempts to do and help them, you've only won something?
Okay.
Yes please. Just be quiet.
I remember when I was a child.
That's the problem of being interfered with.
I just can't remember where I am.
I'm talking about how some people, when they saw Leon Day, getting compensation or getting
anything.
Oh, some of the players thought, why aren't you doing it for me?
What's an impossibility?
You can't help every one of them.
And that's the reason why we help put on card shows and that type of thing.
These things are very expensive to put on and very little money is ever made.
You put on something that cost 10,000 to put on and then you turn over $350 to the player.
I mean, that's how bad it was because Baltimore was not like Chicago or New York or Philadelphia
where there was a better relationship with the media and the press because the guys in
Chicago and Pittsburgh and New York, they knew about the history of it.
They knew they were much more intelligent than our sports casters here.
They read, you know, I didn't have to give them.
I mean, we spent thousands of dollars just in providing books to put on the shelves of
all these guys in the media in this town.
And they looked at the pictures.
I was told and they showed us how to read them.
They looked at the pictures.
So it's that kind of thing in which when you begin to help, you always have the problem
of how far do you go because you've got to get other people involved in it, Seth, in
some way, in to educate them.
And there's the rub.
It's not just like, if I had $100,000 to put into it, you do that.
It is the work that needs to be done along with the money.
And there's no way to compensate that aspect of it because it took months of early...
